perturbation.original_text: All undisputed payments that have accrued prior to the termination or expiration of this Agreement for any reason will be payable in full within thirty (30) days thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: All undisputed payments that have accrued prior to the termination or expiration of this Agreement for any reason will be payable in full within sixty (60) days thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment deadline to 60 days after termination contradicts California law, which requires faster payment of final wages.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code § 201 requires that if an employer discharges an employee, the employee's wages are due and payable immediately. The modified text allows for payment within 60 days, directly contradicting this immediate payment requirement upon termination, as 30 days is no longer in compliance.

EMPLOYMENT REGULATION AND SUPERVISION [200 - 2699.8] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) PART 1. COMPENSATION [200 - 452] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 1. Payment of Wages [200 - 273] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. General Occupations [200 - 244] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 201. (a) If an employer discharges an employee, the wages earned and unpaid at the time of discharge are due and payable immediately. An employer who lays off a group of employees by reason of the termination of seasonal employment in the curing, canning, or drying of any variety of perishable fruit, fish, or vegetables, shall be deemed to have made immediate payment when the wages of said employees are paid within a reasonable time as necessary for computation and payment thereof; provided, however, that the reasonable time shall not exceed 72 hours, and further provided that payment shall be made by mail to any employee who so requests and designates a mailing address therefor. (b) Notwithstanding any other law, the state employer shall be deemed to have made an immediate payment of wages under this section for any unused or accumulated vacation, annual leave, holiday leave, or time off to which the emp
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the payment deadline to 60 days after termination, directly contradicting California Labor Code § 201(a), as stated in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states: "If an employer discharges an employee, the wages earned and unpaid at the time of discharge are due and payable immediately." The change from 30 days to 60 days is a direct violation of the immediate payment requirement upon termination. This creates a clear legal contradiction as the contractual term does not align with the statutory requirement.

perturbation.original_text: Client may, upon no less than thirty (30) days prior written notice to Intuit, cause an independent Certified Public Accountant to inspect all relevant records of Intuit upon which the calculation of Impressions under the Usage Reports are based during Client's normal business hours.
perturbation.changed_text: Client may, upon no less than fifteen (15) days prior written notice to Intuit, cause an independent Certified Public Accountant to inspect all relevant records of Intuit upon which the calculation of Impressions under the Usage Reports are based during Client's normal business hours.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for auditing financial records could be considered unreasonable and impede a proper audit under generally accepted accounting principles and legal standards for financial oversight.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 404 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act requires companies to establish and maintain internal controls over financial reporting. While SOX doesn't specify a minimum notice period for audits, reducing it to 15 days can be seen as hindering the effectiveness of the audit and, by extension, the required internal controls, especially for larger organizations. A short notice period may not allow sufficient time to prepare necessary documentation and personnel, potentially undermining the audit's integrity. This connects to the general principles of SOX compliance, even if not directly violating a specific clause about notice periods.

perturbation.justification: The change from 30 to 15 days notice for an audit creates a potential inconsistency with effective financial oversight, as the explanation says. Although the law snippet mentions SOX and internal controls, the first law snippet is a dead link. The second scraped law snippet provides general context about public company accounting reform, but doesn't explicitly detail notification periods. Despite this, the explanation argues that reducing the notice period impairs the audit's effectiveness. Since the perturbation type is 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' which states that time-sensitive obligations should align with legal requirements and they do not here, it is a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event the parties fail to reach agreement within ten (10) business days following the commencement of such good faith negotiations (or such later date as the parties may agree to), Intuit may offer the opportunity to any third party on terms and conditions no less favorable then those offered to Client.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event the parties fail to reach agreement within five (5) business days following the commencement of such good faith negotiations (or such later date as the parties may agree to), Intuit may offer the opportunity to any third party on terms and conditions no less favorable then those offered to Client.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the negotiation period to five business days might be considered an unreasonably short time to negotiate complex business opportunities, potentially violating the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, especially if significant investment or strategic importance is involved.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-304 states that every contract or duty within the UCC imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. While this contract might not be fully governed by the UCC, the principle of good faith negotiation is widely recognized. Reducing the negotiation period to five days could be seen as acting in bad faith, especially if it's clear that such a short period is insufficient for meaningful negotiation given the complexity and potential value of the sponsorship opportunity. It suggests an intent to quickly move on to other parties without genuinely considering the existing client's position.

perturbation.justification: The original contract provided ten business days for negotiation, while the changed contract reduces this period to five business days. The Law Explanation argues that reducing this period might violate the obligation of good faith, particularly if the reduced timeframe is insufficient for meaningful negotiation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing UCC § 1-304, states that every contract within the UCC imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. The Perturbation Explanation also suggests a potential violation of the duty of good faith and fair dealing. Given these points, reducing the negotiation period raises a strong concern of acting in bad faith, potentially contradicting the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. The change introduces an inconsistency that poses a risk of non-compliance.
